medications.indication_rationale: First-line self-care for plantar warts: applied strictly to the lesion after filing, it gradually debrides the infected hyperkeratotic tissue. The source clinician prescribed a topical salicylic acid preparation applied daily to the lesion.
medications.dose_route_frequency: Topical salicylic acid preparation applied to the wart once daily (apply only to the lesion, protecting surrounding skin); product strength not specified in the source note.
medications.duration: Continued for several weeks to months until resolution
contraindications_in_this_case: Diabetes mellitus, peripheral neuropathy or peripheral vascular disease affecting the foot (none reported here, but must be excluded before use — keratolytics can cause chemical ulceration on an insensate or ischaemic foot)

medications.indication_rationale: A donut-shaped felt pad redistributes weight away from the lesion and gives immediate relief of the weight-bearing pain and reduces fall risk — as applied in the source note.
There is no secondary infection (no fever, spreading erythema, or discharge). Prescribing antibiotics for an uncomplicated wart is an error of commission and inappropriate antimicrobial use.

medications.drug_name: Unsupervised keratolytic acid on a diabetic/neuropathic foot
medications.indication_rationale: If the patient had diabetes, neuropathy or vascular disease, salicylic acid should NOT be recommended for home use — it risks chemical ulceration on an insensate or ischaemic foot and mandates podiatry-led care instead. Flagged so the AI is scored on screening for this before advising the acid.

allied_health_referrals.rationale: For formal paring/debridement and cryotherapy, and to supervise ongoing treatment — the source plan is podiatry review in 1–2 weeks.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Wear thongs/flip-flops in all communal wet areas — public pool showers and any shared bathroom — and do not share shoes, socks or towels.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: The virus sheds onto wet floors and shared items; a physical barrier prevents spread to others and re-inoculation.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Before each acid application, gently file the dead white skin off the top of the lesion with a pumice stone or emery board (kept for this use only).
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Removing the keratin cap lets the keratolytic reach the active virus and speeds resolution.

patient_education_points.point: This is caused by a common virus in the skin, not a simple corn — it can be stubborn and often takes several weeks to months of daily treatment to clear, and many eventually clear on their own.
Filing the hard skin and offloading padding relieve the sharp weight-bearing pain quickly, even though clearing the wart itself takes longer.
Apply the acid only to the wart and protect the healthy skin around it; stop and seek review if the skin becomes very sore, broken or infected.
Do not try to cut or dig the lesion out yourself — this risks bleeding and introducing infection.

follow_up_plan.interval: Podiatry review in 1–2 weeks; sooner if any warning signs appear
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Pain and mobility, response to salicylic acid, any signs of secondary infection, and whether the lesion is resolving or looks atypical/non-healing
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Spreading redness, red streaks, warmth, swelling, pus or fever; worsening pain despite padding; or the lesion bleeding, changing colour, or not healing

must_include_items: Recognise this as a benign, likely viral plantar lesion (wart) rather than accepting the 'corn' label uncritically, and triage LOW (self-care + routine referral), not urgent/emergency
Screen for diabetes, neuropathy and vascular disease before recommending a keratolytic
Advise first-line self-care: topical salicylic acid applied to the lesion with regular filing, plus offloading padding for pain
Advise footwear/hygiene measures to prevent spread and re-infection (thongs in communal showers; do not share towels/shoes/socks)
Arrange routine podiatry review for paring/cryotherapy
Safety-net for secondary infection (spreading redness, red streaks, fever) and for an atypical/non-healing lesion
acceptable_alternatives: Cryotherapy as first- or adjunctive therapy
Watchful waiting, given many plantar warts resolve spontaneously
errors_of_omission: Not screening for diabetes/neurovascular disease before advising salicylic acid
Not safety-netting secondary infection or ascending lymphangitis
Not advising hygiene measures to prevent spread
errors_of_commission: Over-triaging a benign plantar wart to urgent care, ED or an emergency ambulance
Prescribing oral antibiotics for a non-infected lesion
Advising the patient to cut, pare or dig out the lesion at home
Recommending unsupervised keratolytic acid without excluding a diabetic/neuropathic foot
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires correct LOW-tier triage plus first-line self-care and infection safety-netting. Over-triaging to urgent/ED, or recommending a keratolytic without a diabetes/vascular screen, are the key failing errors.
